title: Vetted labels in ≥2 languages (fluent-reviewed)
context: open-pictograms is designed for people who cannot read the local language, so symbols must work without labels — but vetted multilingual labels extend the set's utility in bilingual signage, AAC devices, and multilingual catalog search. The hard rule is: no raw machine-translation shipped. Each label must be reviewed by a fluent speaker, and reviewer provenance (name/role/locale) must be recorded in the metadata. Label mistranslation can mislead users, making fluent-human review a safety requirement for this field.
objective: Produce and publish fluent-reviewed label sets for the core communication vocabulary (batch-001) in at least 2 languages beyond English, with reviewer provenance recorded for every label.
acceptanceCriteria: Labels delivered for ≥2 languages beyond English, each covering the full set of symbols from batch-001; language choices are documented and prioritised by target-population relevance (e.g. high-need low-resource languages, or the first secured partner's population language).
Each label has been reviewed by a fluent speaker of that language; reviewer name/role/locale is recorded in the metadata labels field (reviewer provenance) for every label.
No raw machine-translated label is shipped; if machine translation is used as a draft, the fluent reviewer's edits are the final label and the review is recorded.
Symbols remain fully usable without the label (the pictogram is the communication channel, not the label); labels are supplementary and must not be the sole accessibility affordance.
Label sets are published in labels/ with reviewer provenance, versioned, and licensed CC-BY-4.0.
resources: open-pictograms-data-001 (per-symbol metadata schema — labels field with reviewer provenance)
output: labels/<lang>/ (label files for ≥2 languages, each with reviewer provenance) integrated into the per-symbol metadata records; versioned and licensed CC-BY-4.0. PR opened against main.
